title: Module: endpoints - OS vs PFS vs ORR vs surrogate/biomarker
context: One of the most consequential advocacy skills is understanding that 'the trial showed a benefit' depends entirely on what was measured. Overall survival (OS), progression-free survival (PFS), objective response rate (ORR), and surrogate or biomarker endpoints have fundamentally different relationships to patient well-being. A surrogate gain does not necessarily mean living longer or better. This module is cited in TASKS.md as a key M1 item with specific acceptance criteria around the surrogate-endpoint distinction and FDA/EMA guidance.
objective: Author an original, plain-language educational module distinguishing overall survival, progression-free survival, objective response rate, and surrogate/biomarker endpoints — explaining what each measures, how they relate to patient experience, and why a surrogate gain may not translate to longer or better life.
acceptanceCriteria: Distinguishes overall survival (OS), progression-free survival (PFS), objective response rate (ORR), and surrogate/biomarker endpoints with clear plain-language definitions and the patient-experience relevance of each.

Explains why a gain in a surrogate endpoint (e.g. PFS, ORR, or a biomarker) may not translate to longer or better overall survival, with the FDA/EMA accelerated-approval surrogate framework cited as authority.
Addresses the correlation-but-not-causation problem for surrogates: validated surrogate does not mean proven OS benefit.
Cites FDA oncology endpoint guidance and EMA equivalents; all citations resolve to sources.yaml entries with compatible allowedUse.
Written in plain language; no unexplained jargon without a glossary anchor.
Carries a persistent 'education only — not medical advice' notice and passes the advice-language checklist (task 002).
Passes the licence + provenance build gate and CI.
Signed off by a credentialed oncologist (recorded in the review ledger): endpoint distinctions and surrogate discussion are clinically accurate and nothing reads as treatment guidance.
Signed off by an experienced patient advocate (recorded in the review ledger): content is accessible and does not stray into advice.
Signed off by a methodology reviewer: endpoint definitions and surrogate-correlation discussion are methodologically accurate.
resources: PLAN.md (§ Goals — Goal 2; § Dependencies — FDA/EMA endpoint guidance)
output: A plain-language educational module (content/modules/endpoints.md) committed to the repo, covering OS vs PFS vs ORR vs surrogate endpoints, provenance-cited, CI-green, and carrying dual high-tier sign-off plus methodology review.
